State of California

Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

Technical Assistance • • Administration • • Executive/Legal • • Enforcement • • Statements of Economic Interest
(916) 322-5662         322-5660            322-5901             322-6441               322-6444

March 3, 1981

Robert F. Beyer
City Manager
City of San Rafael
1400 Fifth Avenue
San Rafael, CA  94902

A-81-03-092

Dear Mr. Beyer:

     I apologize for taking so long to respond to your inquiry.
At your request I have gone over Lee Rosenthal's letter
concerning Mr. Nixon.  In general, Mr. Rosenthal has correctly
stated the law.  An official is required to disqualify himself
from any decision if it is "reasonably foreseeable that the
decision will have a material financial effect, distinguishable
from its effect on the public generally" on a source of
income of $250 or more in the previous year.  Government
Code Section 87103.  In applying this standard, Mr. Rosenthal
correctly relied on 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18703.  However,
as Mr. Rosenthal's letter makes clear, it is not always easy
to determine, in a specific factual situation, whether dis-
qualification is necessary.

     As I understand it, no advice is presently being requested
on this set of facts.  We do not normally give advice concerning
decisions which have already been made.

     The remainder of Mr. Rosenthal's letter, and Ms. Cameron's
letter, deal with the disclosure threshold for income to a
business entity in which the filer has a 10 percent or greater
interest.  Ms. Cameron's advice on this subject is correct.

     I hope this answers your questions, and I am sorry for
the delay.  If you have questions concerning the application
of these principles to specific factual situations in the
future, I will be happy to help you.

                                        Very truly yours,

                                        [illegible]
                                        Barbara A. Milman
                                        Chief
                                        Legal Division

BAM:plh

CITY
OF
SAN RAFAEL

1400 FIFTH AVE, SAN RAFAEL, CALIF 94902/PHONE (415) 456-1112

MAYOR
LAWRENCE E. MULRYAN
COUNCIL MEMBERS
DOUGLAS GREENER
FRED JENSEN
JOHN A. MISKIMEN
JACK G. NIXON

January 22, 1981


Ms. Barbara Milman
Conflicts of Interest - Legal Division
State of California Fair Political Practices Division
P.O. Box 807
Sacramento, California  95804

Dear Ms. Milman:

This letter is in follow-up to my conversation with you on
January 22, 1981, asking if you would comment on the enclosed
letter by Mr. Lee C. Rosenthall concerning the matter of potential conflict of interest of Mr. Jack Nixon, Chairman of the
San Rafael Redevelopment Agency.

The purpose of making this inquiry is to ascertain, essentially,
if you agree with the opinion given to Mr. Nixon by Mr. Rosenthall.
I would appreciate any comments you might have on this opinion.
If you could respond prior to January 30th, it would be most
appreciated.

Thank you for your assistance in this matter.

Very truly yours,

[signature]
ROBERT P. BEVIER
City Manager

RPB/lsr

Enclosures

State of California
Fair Political mmission
P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 958... 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

Technical Assistance •• Administration •• Executive/Legal •• Enforcement •• Statements of Economic Interest
(916) 322-5662 322-5660 322-5601 322-6441 322-6444

January 15, 1981

Edmund A. Duggan RECEIVED
San Rafael City Attorney
1400 Fifth Avenue JAN 19 1981
San Rafael, CA 94902
CITY ATTORNEY
CITY OF SAN RAFAEL

Dear Mr. Duggan:

This is in response to your letter of January 9, 1981, in
which you have requested us to confirm the advice you provided
to Jack Nixon, Chairman of the San Rafael Redevelopment Agency,
by memorandum dated January 5, 1980 [sic]. In that memorandum
you advised Mr. Nixon that he is required to disclose clients
who have paid him as little as $250 in the last twelve months.
I am afraid I cannot agree with that conclusion.

It is true that the Political Reform Act requires public
officials to refrain from making, participating in making, or
using their official position to influence any decision which
will have a foreseeable material financial effect distinguishable
from its effect on the public generally, on sources of income
of $250 or more within twelve months prior to the time the
decision is made. Government Code Section 87103. However, with
respect to sources of income to a business entity, assuming a
filer and his spouse own more than 10% of the entity, disclosure
(as distinguished from disqualification) is required only of
those clients and customers of the business entity who pay so
much in gross receipts to the business entity that the filer's
pro rata share is $10,000 or more. This is the disclosure
required of persons specified in Government Code Section 87200
by Government Code Section 87207(b), and Government Code Section
87302(b) provides that the same information shall be disclosed
by designated employees filing under Conflict of Interest Codes,
subject of course to the disclosure categories applicable to
them in the Code. Note that the decision in Hays v. Wood did not
uphold the $1,000 threshold for attorneys and brokers. Con-
sequently, they like all other filers must now disclose at the
$10,000 level.

Mr. Edmund A. Duggan
January 15, 1981
Page Two


Finally, if Mr. Nixon decides that he has a conflict of
interest in a particular decision within the meaning of the
Political Reform Act, his determination not to participate in
that decision must be accompanied by disclosure of the financial
interest, made part of the Redevelopment Agency's official
record. 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18700(b)(5). If he has a
conflict because the decision will have a material financial
effect, distinguishable from its effect on the public generally,
on a source of income of less than $10,000 to his business entity,
then this disclosure at the time of disqualification will result
in the disclosure of the name of a client or customer who was
not required to be reported on his statement of economic interests.

In summary, if Mr. Nixon's pro rata share of gross receipts
paid by a client or customer to a business entity of which he
and his spouse have a 10% or greater interest in between $250
and $10,000, Mr. Nixon is required to disqualify himself from
any decision which will have material financial effect, dis-
tinguishable from its effect on the public generally, on that
source of income, but until he determines, with or without the
advice of counsel, that disqualification is required, he is not
obliged to disclose that client or customer.

If you have any further questions concerning this matter,
please do not hesitate to contact us again.

Sincerely,


Sarah T. Cameron
Deputy Chief for
Conflicts of Interest
Legal Division

STC:nrw
cc: Lee Rosenthal

GOLDEN GATE UNIVERSITY
SAN FRANCISCO & CO

GRADUATE SCHOOL OF
PUBLIC ADMINISTRATION

January 20, 1981

Dear Administrator:

In accordance with the decision of the group participating in the
city and county managers' seminar at GGU, the next discussion will
take place from 10:00 AM - 2:00 PM, on Wednesday, February 4th,
1981, in room 614, with a working lunch and coffee included for the usual $5.00.

The discussion will center around: THE PLACE OF CITIES AND COUNTIES
IN THE ECONOMY OF THE SAN FRANCISCO BAY AREA: Problems & Opportunities.

The discussion leader will be Pat Mason, Chief Economist, ABAG.
Pat directed and conducted the research and data collection and
analysis as well as the drafting of economic development policies
for incorporation into the San Francisco Bay Area Regional Plan.

GGU is on Mission Street between First and Second. Parking is
available and expensive across the street. We are a half-block
from the Eastbay Bus Terminal and one and one-half blocks from the
BART Montgomery Street station.

We hope you will be able to attend this discussion which we have
scheduled to assist you in learning where your agency "fits in" to
the complicated and sophisticated economy of the Bay Area.

Also, please mark your calendars for the following session, March
4th. Bill Hamm, the Legislative Analyst has been invited to let us
know "what cities and counties can expect with reference to $$$$
from the state legislature."

To help order luncheon, please fill in the response below. We
look forward to having you with us.

Yours sincerely,

Randy H. Hamilton, Dean

To: Randy H. Hamilton, Dean Graduate School of Public Administration,
Golden Gate University, 536 Mission Street, San Francisco, CA 94105

I will _____ will not _____ attend the February 4th, 1981, seminar.

Robert F. Beyer
(Name)
City of San Rafael
(City, State of Agency)

GOLDEN GATE UNIVERSITY 536 Mission Street • San Francisco • California 94105 • Telephone (415) 442-7230
Member: National Association of Schools of Public Affairs and Administration International Institute of Administrative Sciences

SRRA AGENDA ITEM #5a
(1/19/81)

LAW OFFICES OF
GOLDFARB & OWENS
STEVEN H. GOLDFARB                           1333 BROADWAY, SUITE 825              TELEPHONE
THOMAS R. OWENS                              OAKLAND, CALIFORNIA 94612             (415) 839-6336
BARRY H. LIPMAN
H. DAVID KROOT
JOYCE S. A TISCHLER
LEE C. ROSENTHAL
R. ZACHARY WASSERMAN

January 13, 1980

Robert Beyer
Executive Director
San Rafael Redevelopment Agency
City Hall
1400 Fifth Avenue
San Rafael, CA 94901

Dear Mr. Beyer:

     At the request of the Agency, I am writing with regard to
the alleged conflict of interest of Agency member Jack Nixon
in the matter of the negative declaration on the 3rd Street
Improvement project.

     The facts as I understand them are as follows: Mr. Nixon
is an insurance salesman. In his insurance business, Mr. Nixon
has received income of more than $250 in the past year from
commissions on the sale of insurance policies to McPhails.
McPhails has a retail business that is located at the corner of
3rd and C Streets. The entrance of McPhails is on C Street, and
it has adequate off-street parking at the rear of its building.

     The decision about which the conflict of interest question
arises involves issuance of a negative declaration under the
California Environmental Quality Act with respect to the 3rd Street
Improvement project. This project involves elimination of about
50 parking spaces on 3rd Street and making certain improvements in
the traffic flow on that street. Of the 50 spaces eliminated,
approximately 15 are within a block of McPhails. The project was
proposed and approved by the Agency in 1978 allegedly without compliance with the California Environmental Quality Act ("CEQA").
Owners of businesses on 3rd Street then brought suit against the
Agency seeking to overturn the approval on grounds that CEQA was
not complied in approving the project. At the request of the owners,
the Superior Court issued a preliminary injunction preventing implementation of the project. The Agency then certified a negative
declaration for the project, and the court thereafter dissolved the
preliminary injunction. After the injunction was dissolved, the Agency
went forward with and completed the project in 1979.

     Trial of the owners' suit was held in 1980, and the trial court

RECEIVED

JAN 13 1981

REDEVELOPMENT AGENCY

Robert Beyer
January 12, 1980
Page 2

judge ordered the Agency to make a new determination as to whether
or not a negative declaration for the project should be issued.
In its order the court specifically ordered the Agency to consider
the elimination of parking on 3rd Street in light of other projects
that have been undertaken to provide off-street parking in the
vicinity of the 3rd Street area. It is this new negative declaration
determination that raises the conflict of interest question for
Mr. Nixon.

Under the Political Reform Act of 1974, an official cannot
participate in a decision if it is "reasonably forseeable that the
decision will have a material financial effect, distinguishable
from its effect on the public generally," on a source of income
from whom the official has received $250 in income in the previous
year. Government Code Section 87103.

Under the peculiar circumstances of this case, it is not reasonably
forseeable that the decision with regard to the negative declaration
will have a material financial effect upon Mr. Nixon's source of
income, McPhails. To begin the decision before the Agency is not the
substantive decision as to whether or not to do the project, but
rather a procedural step, i.e. whether or not to prepare an environmental impact report. That decision standing alone will have no
financial effect on McPhails. In normal circumstances, the decision
whether or not to prepare an environmental impact report is made
as a preliminary step in the process of determining whether to undertake
a project. In those circumstances the decision concerning preparation
of the impact report conceivably could have a material financial
effect if it is reasonably forseeable that the report will impact the
substantive decision regarding the project itself.

In this instance, however, the decision regarding the project
has already been made, and the project itself has been carried out.
Therefore, it is highly unlikely that an environmental impact report
will affect the substantive decision to carry out the project. See
Residents Ad Hoc Stadium Comm. v. Board of Trustees, 89 CA.App.3d 274
(1979).

Therefore, it cannot be said that it is reasonably forseeable
that the decision regarding the negative declaration will affect the
decision with regard to the project in such a manner so as to have
a material financial effect on McPhails.

Even if we were dealing here with the substantive decision to
proceed with the project, it would be difficult to conclude that the
decision would have a material financial effect upon McPhails. The
project eliminated parking on 3rd Street and somewhat altered the
traffic flow on that street. It is not reasonably forseeable that

Robert Beyer
January 13, 1980
Page 3

removal of spaces will materially affect McPhails. The business is
not oriented to 3rd Street but rather to C Street, and it has its
own off-street parking. Thus, it is not foreseeable that the elimination of the few spaces on 3rd Street in the immediate vicinity of
McPhails would materially affect the volume of customers going to
McPhails. Therefore, the income of the business would not be
materially affected.

It should be noted that regulations of the Fair Political Practices
Commission define "material financial effect" as an effect of $100,000
or 15 per year on gross income or $50,000 or 1/20 per year on net
income. 2.Cal.Adm. Code Section 18703. It is highly unlikely that
the elimination of parking on 3rd Street will have the magnitude of
effect on McPhails to be considered material given the fact that
McPhails has sales of five to ten million dollars or more a year.

The other aspect of the project - improvement of traffic flow
in the downtown area of San Rafael - may affect McPhails in that
circulation in the area will be improved. However, all businesses
in the downtown area will be affected in substantially the same manner
by improved circulation. The segment of the population consisting
of all downtown businesses is large enough to be considered the
"public generally" for purposes of determining whether a material
financial effect on a business is distinguishable from the effect
on the public generally. In opinion Requested by William L. Owen,
City Attorney, City of Davis, 2 FPPC Ops.77 (1976). Thus, even if
the traffic aspect of the decision affects McPhails materially, Mr.
Nixon need not disqualify himself since the decision affects all
downtown businesses in the same manner.

With respect to Mr. Duggan's letter of January 5, 1980, to
Mr. Nixon, a comment is appropriate. Contrary to Mr. Duggan's
letter, the California Supreme Court, in Hayes v. Wood, 25 Cal.3d
772 (1979), struck down Government Code Section 87207 (b)(2) as
violative of equal protection and held that the threshold for disclosure of income by brokers and attorneys should be that provided
in Government Code Section 87207 (b)(3) for other professions, i.e.,
$10,000. Thus, contrary to the statements in Mr. Duggan's letter,
Mr. Nixon's threshold for disclosure of a source of income from
his insurance business on a statement of economic interest is
$10,000 in a year, not $1,000 in a year.

In his letter, Mr. Duggan also notes the construction of parking
lots across from McPhails as a possible source of a conflict of
interest. As I understand it, the construction of those lots was
not part of the project for which the original negative declaration
was issued. The effect of those lots obviously relates to the
environmental impact of removal of parking in that the impact of
removal is mitigated by availability of alternative parking. This

Robert Beyer
January 13, 1980
Page 4

issue is discussed in both the 1978 and 1980 initial studies for the
negative declaration. However, I do not see the 1980 negative
declaration decision as being one prepared for the decision to build
that off-street parking. Furthermore, even if the "project" for
which the 1980 negative declaration is prepared is viewed as both
the removal of parking from 3rd Street and the off-street parking,
it is not forseeable that the decision will have a material financial
effect on McPhails. Because the Agency has already purchased the land
for and constructed the lots, it is highly unlikely that a decision
regarding the negative declaration will have any impact on a decision
that has already been made and implemented at a large cost to the
Agency.

Sincerely,

[signature]
LEE C. ROSENTHAL

    William T. Bullard, Jr.

sections.question_synthetic: Does an official need to disqualify themselves from decisions affecting a source of income, and what are the disclosure thresholds for clients/customers of a business entity in which the official has a 10% or greater interest?
sections.conclusion_synthetic: An official must disqualify themselves from decisions with a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on income sources of $250 or more. For business entities where the filer and spouse own more than 30%, disclosure is required only when a client/customer's gross receipts to the entity result in the filer's pro rata share of $10,000 or more. Disqualification may be required for sources between $250-$10,000, but disclosure of that source is not required until disqualification is determined.
sections.parsing_notes: Format: ocr; LLM: Document is OCR-scanned with multiple related letters. Primary FPPC response is from Barbara Milman dated March 3, 1981, with earlier response from Sarah T. Cameron dated January 15, 1981. OCR quality affects confidence; some text is garbled but main legal conclusions are identifiable.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Does an official need to disqualify themselves from decisions affecting a source of income, and what are the disclosure thresholds for clients/customers of a business entity in which the official has a 10% or greater interest?

CONCLUSION: An official must disqualify themselves from decisions with a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on income sources of $250 or more. For business entities where the filer and spouse own more than 30%, disclosure is required only when a client/customer's gross receipts to the entity result in the filer's pro rata share of $10,000 or more. Disqualification may be required for sources between $250-$10,000, but disclosure of that source is not required until disqualification is determined.

BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814 Technical Assistance • • Administration • • Executive/Legal • • Enforcement • • Statements of Economic Interest (916) 322-5662 322-5660 322-5901 322-6441 322-6444 March 3, 1981 Robert F. Beyer City Manager City of San Rafael 1400 Fifth Avenue San Rafael, CA 94902 A-81-03-092 Dear Mr. Beyer: I apologize for taking so long to respond to your inquiry. At your request I have gone over Lee Rosenthal's letter concerning Mr. Nixon. In general, Mr. Rosenthal has correctly stated the law. An official is required to disqualify himself from any decision if it is "reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial effect, distinguishable from its effect on the public generally" on a source of income of $250 or more in the previous year. Government Code Section 87103. In applying this standard, Mr. Rosenthal correctly relied on 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18703. However, as Mr. Rosenthal's letter makes clear, it is not always easy to determine, in a specific factual situation, whether dis- qualification is necessary. As I understand it, no advice is presently being requested on this set of facts. We do not normally give advice concerning decisions which have already been made. The remainder of Mr. Rosenthal's letter, and Ms. Cameron's letter, deal with the disclosure threshold for income to a business entity in which the filer has a 10 percent or greater interest. Ms. Cameron's advice on this subject is correct. I hope this answers your questions, and I am sorry for the delay. If you have questions concerning the application of these principles to specific factual situations in the future, I will be happy to help you. Very truly yours, [illegible] Barbara A. Milman Chief Legal Division BAM:plh CITY OF SAN RAFAEL 1400 FIFTH AVE, SAN RAFAEL, CALIF 94902/PHONE (415) 456-1112 MAYOR LAWRENCE E. MULRYAN COUNCIL MEMBERS DOUGLAS GREENER FRED JENSEN JOHN A. MISKIMEN JACK G. NIXON January 22, 1981 Ms. Barbara Milman Conflicts of Interest - Legal Division State of California Fair Political Practices Division P.O. Box 807 Sacramento, California 95804 Dear Ms. Milman: This letter is in follow-up to my conversation with you on January 22, 1981, asking if you would comment on the enclosed letter by Mr. Lee C. Rosenthall concerning the matter of potential conflict of interest of Mr. Jack Nixon, Chairman of the San Rafael Redevelopment Agency. The purpose of making this inquiry is to ascertain, essentially, if you agree with the opinion given to Mr. Nixon by Mr. Rosenthall. I would appreciate any comments you might have on this opinion. If you could respond prior to January 30th, it would be most appreciated. Thank you for your assistance in this matter. Very truly yours, [signature] ROBERT P. BEVIER City Manager RPB/lsr Enclosures State of California Fair Political mmission P.O. embedding.summary: FPPC responds to inquiries about conflict of interest standards for Jack Nixon, an Agency member, clarifying the disqualification threshold of $250 income and the $10,000 disclosure threshold for business entity clients/customers. The letter addresses the distinction between disqualification requirements and disclosure obligations under the Political Reform Act.
